case_name: In the Matter of James Mann
transcript_text:
This is case 2025-0034 in the matter of James Mann.

Good morning, Attorney Sanderford.

I understand you reserved two minutes for a rebuttal.

Yes, Your Honor.

Please proceed.

Thank you, Your Honor, and just thank you to the court

for allowing us to be heard.

Again, if it pleased the court, I'll begin.

Just identify yourself.

Sure, I apologize, Your Honor.

Again, my name is Colin Sanderford.

I represent the petitioner, Mr. Mann, in this matter.

The question before the court today

involves the interpretation of New Hampshire RSA 651-B6 Roman

numeral VA, talking about the duration of registration,

specifically the passage that we're talking about here

is the one that states that any Tier II or Tier III

offender who was convicted prior to the establishment

of the sex offender registry may petition the court

to be relieved from the registration requirements

under this chapter.

And that court they're referring to

is the Superior Court in the town

where they're residing for someone who's out of state.

And what we're talking about here, Mr. Mann,

is someone who was convicted in Massachusetts prior

to the establishment of the registry in that state.

Right, he was convicted at the time

that New Hampshire had the registry.

That's correct, Your Honor.

So aren't you asking us to add language to the statute?

Well, we're asking you to, I guess,

interpret the statute in such a way, obviously, that

allows Mr. Mann to seek relief.

We believe that the way that the state, given the way

that the statute was, well, the way the statute's written,

coupled with the way in which it came into existence

as a result of this court's finding in Dovey State

back in 2015, in that case the court went through

a rather extensive analysis of the sex offender

registry scheme here in New Hampshire

to ascertain whether or not it had a punitive effect

on the petitioner who was someone who was convicted

in this state prior to the registry.

And at the end of that, the court

found that the sex offender registry system did

have punitive effects, at least as they were applied

to the petitioner in that case.

It laid out a remedy for the resultant ex-post-facto

violation, and it essentially found that the only way

for the act to be constitutionally enforced

against that petitioner, someone who was convicted prior

to the establishment of the registry in this state,

was for that person to be given the opportunity

for a hearing to determine if they needed

to continue registering.

The court laid out the process in Doe

that court should follow until the legislature

essentially enacted that statute,

and the legislature did go in and act 651B6

from an annual VA, 5A.

Doe was an as-applied challenge, right?

That's correct, Your Honor.

And your client has not made an as-applied challenge here?

I don't, no, Your Honor, not specifically to him

as far as that's concerned, but our position is essentially

that when the court left it to the legislature

to fashion the particulars of what this hearing process

would look like, of what this remedy would look like

for folks and for petitioners in Doe's position

at that time, I had also did so with the caveat

that this process would meet the standards

of fundamental fairness.

And part of that.

If we create a system, let's suppose there's a state

with no sex offender registry whatsoever.

We would create a system where non-New Hampshire residents

convicted out of state would have greater opportunity

for relief than New Hampshire residents convicted in state.

Why should we do that from a policy perspective?

So from a policy perspective, first of all,

I don't know that there are any states, territories

or other entities that don't have some form of registry

in place as far as that's concerned,

at least within our, again, like I said,

within our national system here,

within the United States and our territories.

Second of all, there certainly may be ways

for, again, for the state to go in and, you know,

the state can certainly look to certain convictions.

I believe that's what the state does now

to essentially find out if those are analogous

to whatever laws we have here

and it makes that determination.

Again, like I said, I don't know if the example

is necessarily one that we would,

from a policy perspective, that we'd have to worry about

because I don't know that you're going to find

any governmental entities that don't have

a registry system in place as far as that's concerned.

As to the language of the statute,

Section 1B6, Roman 5A refers to the establishment

of the sex offender registry.

Isn't the use of the, I mean, the New Hampshire

sex offender registry?

It could be read that way, certainly, Your Honor.

And, but it's-

Wouldn't the legislature have used A instead of B?

It could have, Your Honor.

You know, the fact that it didn't,

I guess may indicate some sort of intent

as far as that's concerned,

but I would note that the legislature had

the benefit of having the DOE case

and the court's reasoning and the court's analysis

and the court's language where it pointed out

that the issue with the registry system

was the lifetime review without registration,

they specifically said.

Absent this lifetime review without registration,

we think it's okay.

But this lifetime review without registration provision

was where the court said it becomes punitive

and it becomes so punitive that it becomes unconstitutional.

And the court didn't say that, again,

and for what it's worth,

the court didn't necessarily say that lifetime review

without registration is necessarily a bad thing

or is necessarily unconstitutional.

Indeed, people convicted today

are subject to lifetime review

without the ability to be removed from that list.

The difference between people convicted today

or defendants convicted after the establishment

of the registry in their own state

is that those people are all noticed

at the time of their conviction

that one of the main collateral consequences

of that conviction.

I wanna get back to the statute

because it's the immediate issue before us.

Another section of the same chapter,

B11, Roman I,

uses the same phrase,

the sex offender registry.

Are you familiar with that?

I don't necessarily have that one committed to memory.

What I would say is that...

But that phrase in the context of that paragraph

of chapter 651B,

it clearly refers to these sex offender registry

in New Hampshire because it's talking about

defraying the cost of the registry here in New Hampshire.

Certainly are.

You agree with that?

I do, I would agree with that.

It's certainly to the extent

that that's how the court's reading it again.

I just, I can't, like I said,

I don't have it committed to memory.

What I would offer though,

when we're talking about the provision

that we're getting into,

what we're talking about is removal from the registry.

And as we noted,

in Doe, the court said that is the,

that's the main issue.

That's the reason that the court said

something's gotta be done about this registry.

That's why the legislature has to go back

and enact legislation to essentially save the act.

Otherwise, we know, you know,

the court said we know you don't want us

to just completely vitiate it

and we're not going to go back

and tell you which parts of the act are too punitive.

So instead we're gonna...

The client has the ability to petition for removal.

He does, he doesn't, Your Honor.

He can petition for removal from the public list.

Correct.

But that's not removal from the registration requirement.

And that's, it's the ability to be relieved

from the registration requirement

that was at issue in Doe.

And the plaintiff in Doe is the issue in Doe, again,

is that the plaintiff there was not aware,

again, had not been given notice.

That's what, when the court talks about the remedy

that it was fashioning,

the common denominators in sort of what it discusses there

is the idea of notice and a process for a remedy.

And it talks about that in other contexts.

It talks about DCYF case and property tax issues there.

You haven't raised a constitutional claim in this appeal.

You're referring to Doe, but you,

I mean, where's the argument that

that there's a violation of the constitution?

Well, again, I guess it's just the,

my lack of artful drafting there

as far as that's concerned, Your Honor.

And perhaps I'm taking too much of a simplistic look at this.

Again, our position is that someone,

the legislature went out and crafted the statute as it did

and it talked about the establishment

of the sex offender registry.

The legislature knows, as we see in other parts

of the statute there, that there will be others coming,

that there will likely be other people coming

from other states because it refers to the fact

that people who are convicted in other states

will have to register.

So when we think about the idea

that then there's going to be this enactment

that says people who were convicted

prior to the establishment of the registry

have the ability to seek relief

from the registration requirements,

it would follow then that the registry

that would be controlling in that situation

would be the home state registry.

Again, in a situation such as this,

Mr. Mann was not on notice at the time of his conviction

that there would be any collateral consequences.

There would be any registration,

whether in Massachusetts or anywhere else

as far as that's concerned.

So given that, then it seems clear

that what the court was wanting there is again,

someone should at least be on notice

that they would have to register for life.

And if they weren't on notice of that

whenever they were convicted,

they should at least be allowed the opportunity

to be heard and to be removed from the registry.

Doesn't say that they have to be removed

from the registry.

They at least need an opportunity to be heard

whether the court can at least make a determination

as to whether or not they're a danger.

In this situation, the court could have certainly heard

Mr. Mann's case and just denied him

the relief in question.

So we're not saying that he's entitled to the relief

just the hearing as far as that's concerned.

I do want to point out the state has some concern.

The state did note some concerns in its response

that somehow this would lead to, again,

additional people coming from other states

that would seek relief under our reading of the statute.

And while that might be the case,

that's a finite number of people

that's not going to get any larger.

There won't be any other people

that are convicted prior to the establishment

of any registries.

I don't think they're, like I said,

I don't think there are any states

where there are no registries.

So that's a number of people

that's only going to get smaller as the years go by.

It's not going to, that won't be increased.

And the other piece is that, again,

in practice, to the extent that this is read

to essentially read that the registry in question

would be the registry for the state of conviction,

it essentially just be one extra line

in a petition to seek removal from the registry,

whereby someone would just point out

that they are eligible for relief

because they were convicted in a certain state

and that state established a registry in a certain year

and they were convicted prior to that.

So it's not going to lead to anything

that's going to be challenging

for course to be able to ascertain

and it's not going to lead to any sort of influx

in my mind that we would see

as far as additional people coming from other states

that were convicted prior to, in the mid 1990s and before,

come out here somehow coming to seek relief

and like I said, that number of people

is not going to get any larger.

So from a policy perspective,

the state's concerns are minimal compared to

when we compare those to Mr. Mann's rights

and to what he's facing and his inability

to seek any kind of relief

even though he was convicted prior

to the establishment of the registry in Massachusetts

for what it's worth,

he has been removed from that requirement

in Massachusetts.

And now he just seeks the opportunity

to seek that same relief here in New Hampshire.

Thank you.

Thank you.

Reserved minutes.

Attorney Carroll.

May it please the court?

Attorney Carroll, Collin Carroll

for the Department of Safety

through the Department of Justice.

This appeal today is a simple matter

of statutory interpretation,

namely RSA 651B room in numeral five section A.

The trial court below found that the term

of the use of the sex offender registry

had the singular indefinite article that preceded it,

which led to the clear and unambiguous reading

that the term the sex offender registry

under that statute is referring

to the New Hampshire sex offender registry.

As this court had just pointed out,

the term the sex offender registry

is used also in RSA 651B11, Roman numeral run.

And I would also point out that under that statute,

the one that issues 651B6 Roman numeral five,

just in the subsequent sentences in that paragraph,

the legislature is distinguishing the sex offender

from New Hampshire to the sex offender registries

in those foreign jurisdictions.

And so taking that context,

not just the plain language of the statute,

but also the context of the paragraph at whole,

it's clear that the legislature intended that to be,

and the language is clear that it should refer

to the New Hampshire sex offender registry.

The appellant is essentially asking this court

to read words into the statute.

And what he's asking this court to do

is subordinate New Hampshire's registration relief

requirements to those requirements

of those foreign jurisdictions

based on their action as to when

they, those registries were established.

In this instance, the petitioner,

the New Hampshire sex offender registry

was established in 1992.

The petitioner was convicted in 1995.

And so there is no dispute

that he does not qualify as a tier one

or tier two offender who was convicted

prior to the establishment of the sex offender registry.

To the extent the petitioner is arguing

that because of his similar situation

to the petitioner and dough,

he's entitled to the statutory relief.

This is a statutory petition

that is asking for statutory relief

from the statutory scheme.

As this court had pointed out,

the petitioner and dough was bringing forth

an as applied constitutional challenge.

And that analysis that was present in that case

is simply not here.

In any case, this court need not read words

of the statute when the petitioner

can simply bring forth another

declaratory judgment action

and bring forth those arguments

similar to one in dough.

It would be inappropriate for this court

to do the intense effects analysis done in dough

when it simply has not been briefed for the lower court.

It hasn't been brought on the notice of appeal

and to the extent that it's raised at all

whatsoever in the appellant's brief.

It hasn't been adequately briefed

the states would be prejudiced

to provide an analysis in that regard.

The lower court was correct in finding that

to the extent that there's any relevance

of the similar situation of dough,

which as I alluded to earlier,

the sole qualifier for relief under the statute

is being convicted prior to the establishment

of the New Hampshire sex offender registry.

To the extent there is any similar situation with dough

it's not relevant to the statutory relief here.

And the lower court correctly found

and there's no dispute here

that the petitioner here has a different set of facts,

different relief available to him.

Whereas in dough there was no relief

for removal from the public list.

The petitioner here can petition

to have that removal from the public list.

But what about his argument?

He's still required to register for life.

He may still be required to register for life

but to the extent that he's now coming

and making the argument that is unconstitutional

as applied to me, he simply just has not briefed the issue

and nothing would foreclose him

from allowing him to make those arguments

and bring forth the declaratory judgment.

So here it's just a matter of the petitioner

is in a different situation

as the petitioner in dough, there's no dispute there.

There is different relief available to him

and the state's simply asking this court

not to reword into the statute

where the language is clear and unambiguous.

It's referring to the sex offender registry

and I think there's unique public safety concerns

in the state of New Hampshire

when you look at the state of Massachusetts

we're a very different state.

We have very different legislature

and so we have unique public safety concerns.

We restrict the request not to allow offenders

to come into New Hampshire and say

I'm gonna carry those laws

from that foreign jurisdiction with us

when it simply was not contemplated in the statute.

It's not contemplated under the statutory

relief requirements.

What's your response to the argument

in Mr. Mann's brief that the tier three

of just reading the tier three offenders

such as the one in dough

has a chance to seek greater relief

than Mr. Mann, a tier two offender in a similar position?

And I take that to mean that a tier three offender

under the statutory scheme has the ability

to be or has the right to seek removal

from the registration or excuse

from the registration requirement

whereas a tier two offender would not.

Thank you.

To the extent there's any difference here

it's simply based on that factual distinction

which is the petitioner in dough was convicted in 1987.

The New Hampshire sex offender industry

was established in 1992

whereas the petitioner here was convicted

or pled in 1995.

And so in dough you're dealing

with an ex post-facso constitutional analysis

where this registration relief is required

to uphold the constitutionality of the statute

whereas in here that those same circumstances

are not present.

In this instance, prior to Mr. Mann's conviction

the New Hampshire sex offender registry

was published in public law

and so therefore that same remedy

is not necessitated here under the statute.

Of course there's no further questions

so I would rest on my briefs.

Thank you.

Thank you very much.

Thank you Carol.

To the bottom.

Again as noted the fact that Mr. Mann

has somehow can seek the middling relief

to be removed from the public list

should not preclude him from seeking

the removal from the list as far as that's concerned.

Bottom line is this court has found

that subjecting someone to lifetime registration

without the ability to be reviewed

when that person was convicted prior

to the establishment of the sex offender registry

when that person was not on notice

that they would be required to register is a problem.

Well how is he not on notice of New Hampshire's registry?

He was a Massachusetts resident.

Nothing would stop him from knowing

if he had a goal of living in New Hampshire one day

or he had property in New Hampshire.

Nothing would deprive him of the opportunity

to know New Hampshire's law if that was important to him.

I think it would be unreasonable

to expect a defendant in one state

to go and ascertain the collateral consequences

that they may face in 50 other states

and any number of territories or other jurisdictions

as far as that's concerned.

And then know that and somehow

and then make those decisions.

I think that that would be unworkable.

Again, the bottom line is Mr. Mann was convicted

prior to the establishment of the registry in Massachusetts.

The court made it very clear

that that was really the problem there.

That's what they laid out to the legislature.

This is the problem.

Work on this idea of registration

or of registration without any type of review.

Mr. Mann was convicted, again,

he was convicted prior to the establishment of the registry

was not required to register

until actually his sentence was completed in 2002.

So again, while his facts

may be somewhat different from the plaintiff in Doe,

the bottom line is he was required to register

without ever having been put on notice of that.

And the way that the statute,

we should read the statute in such a way

that it winds up being constitutional

under the mandates of Doe.

At a minimum, isn't he presumed

to know the requirements of New Hampshire law

when he moves to the state?

Certainly, yes, certainly there is that, Your Honor.

But again, that was not until many years later

after the conviction.

So again, that is certainly a part of it

to the extent that he chooses to move here.

He may have to register

and he may have to go through this process.

But at the time of his conviction,

he wouldn't have known that.

And like I said, he would have had to have gone out

and known 49 other states

and also a number of territories.

And that would just be, again,

unreasonable to think that someone's

wanted to get into that

when they're in the middle of plea negotiations

or trial as far as that's concerned.

Thank you very much, Attorney Sanderford.

Thank you, Council.

The case is submitted.

Court invites a council in

segments.text: This is case 2025-0034 in the matter of James Mann.
Good morning, Attorney Sanderford.
I understand you reserved two minutes for a rebuttal.
Yes, Your Honor.
Please proceed.
Thank you, Your Honor, and just thank you to the court
for allowing us to be heard.
Again, if it pleased the court, I'll begin.
Just identify yourself.
Sure, I apologize, Your Honor.
Again, my name is Colin Sanderford.
I represent the petitioner, Mr. Mann, in this matter.
The question before the court today
involves the interpretation of New Hampshire RSA 651-B6 Roman
numeral VA, talking about the duration of registration,
specifically the passage that we're talking about here
is the one that states that any Tier II or Tier III
offender who was convicted prior to the establishment
of the sex offender registry may petition the court
to be relieved from the registration requirements
under this chapter.
And that court they're referring to
is the Superior Court in the town
where they're residing for someone who's out of state.
And what we're talking about here, Mr. Mann,
is someone who was convicted in Massachusetts prior
to the establishment of the registry in that state.
Right, he was convicted at the time
that New Hampshire had the registry.
That's correct, Your Honor.
So aren't you asking us to add language to the statute?
Well, we're asking you to, I guess,
interpret the statute in such a way, obviously, that
allows Mr. Mann to seek relief.
We believe that the way that the state, given the way
that the statute was, well, the way the statute's written,
coupled with the way in which it came into existence
as a result of this court's finding in Dovey State
back in 2015, in that case the court went through
a rather extensive analysis of the sex offender
registry scheme here in New Hampshire
to ascertain whether or not it had a punitive effect
on the petitioner who was someone who was convicted
in this state prior to the registry.
And at the end of that, the court
found that the sex offender registry system did
have punitive effects, at least as they were applied
to the petitioner in that case.
It laid out a remedy for the resultant ex-post-facto
violation, and it essentially found that the only way
for the act to be constitutionally enforced
against that petitioner, someone who was convicted prior
to the establishment of the registry in this state,
was for that person to be given the opportunity
for a hearing to determine if they needed
to continue registering.
The court laid out the process in Doe
that court should follow until the legislature
essentially enacted that statute,
and the legislature did go in and act 651B6
from an annual VA, 5A.
Doe was an as-applied challenge, right?
That's correct, Your Honor.
And your client has not made an as-applied challenge here?
I don't, no, Your Honor, not specifically to him
as far as that's concerned, but our position is essentially
that when the court left it to the legislature
to fashion the particulars of what this hearing process
would look like, of what this remedy would look like
for folks and for petitioners in Doe's position
at that time, I had also did so with the caveat
that this process would meet the standards
of fundamental fairness.
And part of that.
If we create a system, let's suppose there's a state
with no sex offender registry whatsoever.
We would create a system where non-New Hampshire residents
convicted out of state would have greater opportunity
for relief than New Hampshire residents convicted in state.
Why should we do that from a policy perspective?
So from a policy perspective, first of all,
I don't know that there are any states, territories
or other entities that don't have some form of registry
in place as far as that's concerned,
at least within our, again, like I said,
within our national system here,
within the United States and our territories.
Second of all, there certainly may be ways
for, again, for the state to go in and, you know,
the state can certainly look to certain convictions.
I believe that's what the state does now
to essentially find out if those are analogous
to whatever laws we have here
and it makes that determination.
Again, like I said, I don't know if the example
is necessarily one that we would,
from a policy perspective, that we'd have to worry about
because I don't know that you're going to find
any governmental entities that don't have
a registry system in place as far as that's concerned.
As to the language of the statute,
Section 1B6, Roman 5A refers to the establishment
of the sex offender registry.
Isn't the use of the, I mean, the New Hampshire
sex offender registry?
It could be read that way, certainly, Your Honor.
And, but it's-
Wouldn't the legislature have used A instead of B?
It could have, Your Honor.
You know, the fact that it didn't,
I guess may indicate some sort of intent
as far as that's concerned,
but I would note that the legislature had
the benefit of having the DOE case
and the court's reasoning and the court's analysis
and the court's language where it pointed out
that the issue with the registry system
was the lifetime review without registration,
they specifically said.
Absent this lifetime review without registration,
we think it's okay.
But this lifetime review without registration provision
was where the court said it becomes punitive
and it becomes so punitive that it becomes unconstitutional.
And the court didn't say that, again,
and for what it's worth,
the court didn't necessarily say that lifetime review
without registration is necessarily a bad thing
or is necessarily unconstitutional.
Indeed, people convicted today
are subject to lifetime review
without the ability to be removed from that list.
The difference between people convicted today
or defendants convicted after the establishment
of the registry in their own state
is that those people are all noticed
at the time of their conviction
that one of the main collateral consequences
of that conviction.
I wanna get back to the statute
because it's the immediate issue before us.
Another section of the same chapter,
B11, Roman I,
uses the same phrase,
the sex offender registry.
Are you familiar with that?
I don't necessarily have that one committed to memory.
What I would say is that...
But that phrase in the context of that paragraph
of chapter 651B,
it clearly refers to these sex offender registry
in New Hampshire because it's talking about
defraying the cost of the registry here in New Hampshire.
Certainly are.
You agree with that?
I do, I would agree with that.
It's certainly to the extent
that that's how the court's reading it again.
I just, I can't, like I said,
I don't have it committed to memory.
What I would offer though,
when we're talking about the provision
that we're getting into,
what we're talking about is removal from the registry.
And as we noted,
in Doe, the court said that is the,
that's the main issue.
That's the reason that the court said
something's gotta be done about this registry.
That's why the legislature has to go back
and enact legislation to essentially save the act.
Otherwise, we know, you know,
the court said we know you don't want us
to just completely vitiate it
and we're not going to go back
and tell you which parts of the act are too punitive.
So instead we're gonna...
The client has the ability to petition for removal.
He does, he doesn't, Your Honor.
He can petition for removal from the public list.
Correct.
But that's not removal from the registration requirement.
And that's, it's the ability to be relieved
from the registration requirement
that was at issue in Doe.
And the plaintiff in Doe is the issue in Doe, again,
is that the plaintiff there was not aware,
again, had not been given notice.
That's what, when the court talks about the remedy
that it was fashioning,
the common denominators in sort of what it discusses there
is the idea of notice and a process for a remedy.
And it talks about that in other contexts.
It talks about DCYF case and property tax issues there.
You haven't raised a constitutional claim in this appeal.
You're referring to Doe, but you,
I mean, where's the argument that
that there's a violation of the constitution?
Well, again, I guess it's just the,
my lack of artful drafting there
as far as that's concerned, Your Honor.
And perhaps I'm taking too much of a simplistic look at this.
Again, our position is that someone,
the legislature went out and crafted the statute as it did
and it talked about the establishment
of the sex offender registry.
The legislature knows, as we see in other parts
of the statute there, that there will be others coming,
that there will likely be other people coming
from other states because it refers to the fact
that people who are convicted in other states
will have to register.
So when we think about the idea
that then there's going to be this enactment
that says people who were convicted
prior to the establishment of the registry
have the ability to seek relief
from the registration requirements,
it would follow then that the registry
that would be controlling in that situation
would be the home state registry.
Again, in a situation such as this,
Mr. Mann was not on notice at the time of his conviction
that there would be any collateral consequences.
There would be any registration,
whether in Massachusetts or anywhere else
as far as that's concerned.
So given that, then it seems clear
that what the court was wanting there is again,
someone should at least be on notice
that they would have to register for life.
And if they weren't on notice of that
whenever they were convicted,
they should at least be allowed the opportunity
to be heard and to be removed from the registry.
Doesn't say that they have to be removed
from the registry.
They at least need an opportunity to be heard
whether the court can at least make a determination
as to whether or not they're a danger.
In this situation, the court could have certainly heard
Mr. Mann's case and just denied him
the relief in question.
So we're not saying that he's entitled to the relief
just the hearing as far as that's concerned.
I do want to point out the state has some concern.
The state did note some concerns in its response
that somehow this would lead to, again,
additional people coming from other states
that would seek relief under our reading of the statute.
And while that might be the case,
that's a finite number of people
that's not going to get any larger.
There won't be any other people
that are convicted prior to the establishment
of any registries.
I don't think they're, like I said,
I don't think there are any states
where there are no registries.
So that's a number of people
that's only going to get smaller as the years go by.
It's not going to, that won't be increased.
And the other piece is that, again,
in practice, to the extent that this is read
to essentially read that the registry in question
